narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of failing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 40, Year 1 school decile ranking of 1st (well below the typical 6th decile), Year 3 decile of 2nd (also below the average 6th), and your plan to attend full-time—and calculated a 40.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. Because this risk threshold was too high, your application was rejected to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing. The three most influential factors in this decision were your LSAT score, your Year 3 decile ranking, and your Year 1 decile ranking. Your LSAT score of 40, which stands out above the average of 37, was the strongest positive signal, significantly pushing the prediction toward you passing the bar rather than failing. In contrast, your low Year 3 decile ranking of 2nd worked against you, nudging the model toward a higher failure risk compared to peers who typically rank around the 6th decile. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1st had a comparable negative pull, highlighting early academic struggles that deviated from the norm and raised concerns about long-term performance. While your full-time status aligns with most applicants and your GPA is close to average, these top factors overshadowed them in the model's eyes. Overall, your situation compares to typical students by having a solid LSAT but weaker high school decile rankings that signal potential hurdles in sustaining performance through law school and the bar. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to further boost your score, seeking academic advising to improve your rankings or equivalent metrics in future applications, and targeting schools with more flexible admission criteria or strong bar prep support. With targeted improvements, you can reshape your profile for better outcomes next time.
